1:
"And he entered again into the synagogue; and there was a man there which had a withered hand." The witnesses set the scene, probe the man's condition, and read the healing both historically and mystically.

The setting, Gill and Wesley note, was probably the Capernaum synagogue, on another sabbath after the dispute over the disciples plucking corn (Matthew 12:9). The grammatical-historical commentators examine the hand: it was, says Robertson, "in a withered state" (a perfect passive participle), "showing that it was not congenital, but the result of injury by accident or disease" — the right hand, Luke adds (Luke 6:6). Theophylact frames the occasion: having confounded the Jews over the cornfields, the Lord "now further brings them to the truth" by working "a miracle on the sabbath," showing that what is lawful for the body's need is no violation of the day.

Henry's tradition draws the pastoral note: the patient's case "was piteous; he had a withered hand, by which he was disabled to work for his living," and "those that are so are the most proper objects of charity." The deed was meant to confirm "that it is lawful to do good on the sabbath day."

The fathers add a mystical reading. Bede sees in "the man with a withered hand... the human race, dried up as to its fruitfulness in good works": "the hand of man, which in our first parent had been dried up when he plucked the fruit of the forbidden tree," is "through the grace of the Redeemer, who stretched his guiltless hands on the tree of the cross... restored to health." He notes too that "well was it in the synagogue that the hand was withered, for where the gift of knowledge is greater, there also the danger of inexcusable guilt is greater." Theophylact reads the withered right hand as the soul no longer doing "the works which belong to the right side," bidden to "arise from sin" and "stand in the midst"; while Pseudo-Jerome applies it to "the avaricious, who, being able to give, had rather receive," now commanded to labour "that they may have to give to him that needeth" (Ephesians 4:28).
2:
"And they watched him, whether he would heal him on the sabbath day; that they might accuse him." The witnesses dwell on the malice of the watchers and the trap they were laying.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the verb closely. "They watched" (paretēroun) is an imperfect, says Robertson, "were watching on the side (or sly)" — Clarke adds "they maliciously watched him" — while Luke's middle voice accents "their personal interest in the proceedings" (cf. Luke 14:1; Luke 6:7). They were, Robertson notes, "probably the same Pharisees" who had disputed in the wheat fields the previous sabbath, "ready to catch him in the act if he should dare to violate their rules." Gill identifies them as "the ruler of the synagogue, and the principal men in it; particularly the Scribes and Pharisees, who followed him wherever he went."

Their purpose was wholly hostile. The watching was "that they might accuse him" — Luke putting it that they sought "how to accuse him" — so that, as Robertson observes, "they were determined to accuse him," and "the sabbath controversy offered the best opening." Their interest in the withered man was not compassion but the hope of a charge.

Wesley traces the deepening of their hatred: "pride, anger, and shame, after being so often put to silence, began now to ripen into malice." The witnesses thus present the verse as a portrait of settled enmity — men in the house of God on the day of God, not to seek mercy for a sufferer, but to convert a work of mercy into an accusation. Their very vigilance, the commentators imply, condemned them: they watched the Healer, hoping he would heal, that the healing might be turned into a crime; and so they made the sabbath, given for man's good, into a snare against the doing of good. Barnes notes the parallel watching in Luke 14:1, where the same spirit dogs Jesus at a Pharisee's table; the malice was not a passing mood but a fixed campaign, dressed in the appearance of zeal for the law even as it plotted the ruin of the Lord of the law.
3:
"And he saith unto the man which had the withered hand, Stand forth." The witnesses read the command as a bold and deliberate public confrontation of the spies.

Robertson renders it "step into the middle of the room where all can see," and calls it "a bold defiance of the Christ's spying enemies"; Wycliff's "they aspieden him," he notes, captures how "they played the spy on Jesus," so that "one can see the commotion among the long-bearded hypocrites at this daring act." Gill adds that Christ, "knowing their thoughts" (so Luke, Luke 6:8) — their "reasonings and designs" — turned from reasoning with them "from the lesser to the greater" and addressed the man directly.

The fathers give the placing in the midst a purpose of mercy. Christ "placed him in the midst," says Bede (with the Antiochene tradition), "that they might be frightened at the sight, and on seeing him compassionate him, and lay aside their malice" — anticipating "the calumny of the Jews" by exposing their own hardness. The sufferer set openly before the assembly was meant to move the watchers to pity, so that "for shame" they might "not account his cure a crime" (so Henry's tradition).

Theophylact hears in "stand forth" a spiritual summons. To the soul whose hand is withered through forbidden deeds Christ says "Arise" — that is, "from sin" — "and stand in the midst," that the hand "may stretch itself forth neither too little nor too much," restored to the measure of virtue. The verse, slight in its words, thus carries for the witnesses a double weight: the courage of Christ, who will not heal in a corner but in the open face of his enemies; and the call to every withered soul to come forth from concealment into the light, where alone the healing word is spoken. Gill observes that Christ acts only after reasoning with them "from the lesser to the greater," appealing to their own practice of rescuing a beast fallen into a pit on the sabbath; the man bidden to stand forth becomes the living test of whether they will own that a human sufferer is worth more than their rule.
4:
"Is it lawful to do good on the sabbath days, or to do evil? to save life, or to kill? But they held their peace." The witnesses dwell on the piercing alternative and the silence it produced.

The question, the commentators agree, fixes a maxim. "It seems to have been a maxim with the Jews," says Barnes, "that not to do good when we have an opportunity was to do evil; not to save life was to kill"; for "if a man has an opportunity of saving a man's life... and does not do it, he is evidently guilty of his death." Clarke states it as a principle "for all men": "he who neglected to preserve life when it was in his power was to be reputed a murderer." Barnes presses the application beyond the moment — "the ability to do good imposes an obligation to do it" — so that whoever has the means "of feeding the hungry... and that does it not, is... practically doing evil" (cf. Matthew 25:45). Robertson calls the alternatives "pitiless," "to save a life or to kill," "a terrible exposure."

"But they held their peace." Their silence the witnesses read as defeat, not assent. It was, says Robertson, "sullen silence and helplessness before the merciless questions of Jesus as the poor man stood there before them all." Wesley judges them "confounded, though not convinced" — silenced "being confounded," not persuaded. Lightfoot recalls a rabbinic parallel of the Sanhedrin who, judging a powerful man's servant, "dared not to utter their opinion" but "fixed their eyes upon the earth."

The witnesses thus find the verse a model of how the Lord answers a hostile question — not by evasion, but by a counter-question that lays bare the questioners' own hearts. The Pharisees had come to accuse him of breaking the sabbath by healing; he shows that their refusal to do good, and their plotting to destroy, was the true sabbath-breaking. To withhold the cure would have been the evil; to give it, the good the day was made for. Gill notes the question is pressed home upon "the Scribes and Pharisees, who were watching him," answering their unspoken charge with one they cannot escape; and Robertson observes that the alternatives admit no third way, so that their silence is itself a confession that the good they forbade was the very thing the law required.
5:
"And when he had looked round about on them with anger, being grieved for the hardness of their hearts, he saith unto the man, Stretch forth thine hand. And he stretched it out: and his hand was restored." The witnesses dwell on the look of anger-and-grief, the hardness of heart, and the cure by a word.

The look is one of several Mark records (so Robertson notes), and it is anger tempered by grief. The anger, all agree, was sinless. Wesley fixes "the true standard of Christian anger": "angry at the sin, grieved at the sinner," adding that none can separate the two "but a true believer in Christ." Barnes calls it "hatred of the sin... joined with the extreme grief" at their state, "not... revengeful passion"; Robertson, that "anger against wrong as wrong is a sign of moral health," the indignant look consistent with "the love and pity of Jesus, the Man of Sorrows." Calvin, for the Reformed, makes it a doctrine: "this was a just and holy anger," proof "that Christ was not free from human passions," and so "that the passions themselves are not sinful, provided there be no excess." Yet with us "our anger, even when it rests on proper grounds, is never free from sin," so "we ought to implore... the Spirit of God to correct our excesses."

The object of his grief was "the hardness of their hearts." The word (pōrōsis), Robertson explains, denotes "moral ossification... like hardened hands or feet"; Clarke gathers the variant readings — "callousness," "deadness," "blindness" — to express "the fullness of this people's wretchedness," hearts "past feeling" by "a long resistance to the grace and Spirit of God." Barnes warns that the heart "is made hard by indulgence in sin; by long resisting the offers of life," and "most tender in youth."

The cure follows by a word alone. He bids the man "stretch forth thine hand," and "power goes along with the word of Christ" (Henry's tradition): though our hands be spiritually "withered, and we cannot of ourselves stretch them forth, we must attempt it," and "if we do our endeavour, power... effects the cure." The man's obedience and instant restoring became, for the witnesses, the pattern of grace working through the command it gives.
6:
"And the Pharisees went forth, and straightway took counsel with the Herodians against him, how they might destroy him." The witnesses mark the murderous plot, the strange alliance, and Christ's withdrawal.

The grammatical-historical commentators register the speed and savagery. Stung beyond endurance, the Pharisees "stalked out at once in a rage of madness" (Robertson, citing Luke 6:11) and, outside the synagogue, "took counsel... how they might destroy him" — a striking inversion, Robertson notes, of the alternative Jesus had just posed, "to save life or to kill." Barnes lays bare the motive: "they hated him for his holiness; because he reproved them... and because he won the hearts of the people," and so "determined to remove him." Henry's tradition observes that "such a work of mercy should have engaged their love," but instead the Pharisees and Herodians, "of opposite interests one to another, took counsel together against him."

The Herodians, here first named, the commentators identify variously. Bede takes them as "the servants of Herod the Tetrarch," who "on account of the hatred which their lord had for John, pursued... the Saviour also." Theophylact reports a sharper view: "a certain new heresy had sprung up, which asserted that Herod was the Christ," since the Jewish princes had failed and an alien ruled (cf. Matthew 22:16).

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the alliance as a parable of ungodliness. The Pharisees "regarded the Herodians with the fiercest hatred," yet "this aversion is counteracted by their hatred and fury against Christ, which makes them... give their hand" to those they despised: "while ungodliness hurries men in various directions... it engages them, with one consent, in a contest with God. No hostilities prevent them from giving their hand to each other for opposing the truth of God." Bede adds that Jesus "withdrew himself... to the sea," fleeing "their treachery, because the hour of his passion had not yet come," and so giving his disciples "an example, when they suffer persecution in one city, to flee to another." Those who suffer for doing good, says Henry's tradition, "do but suffer as their Master did."
7:
"But Jesus withdrew himself with his disciples to the sea: and a great multitude from Galilee followed him." The witnesses read the withdrawal as prudence, not fear, and mark the crowds that gathered.

Jesus withdrew, the commentators agree, because he knew of the plot. "Evidently Jesus knew of the plot to kill him," says Robertson, "perceiving it" (Matthew 12:15); "he and his would be safer by the open beach." Gill stresses it was "not out of fear, but because his time was not yet come, and he had more work to do"; Barnes draws the lesson that "he prudently took care of his life, thus showing that we are not needlessly to throw ourselves into danger." Robertson notes this is one of some eleven occasions on which Mark records the withdrawals of Jesus — "to escape his enemies, for prayer, for rest, for private conference with his disciples."

Yet the withdrawal did not lessen his following. "As often, a great multitude from Galilee followed him" (Robertson); the enmity of the rulers, Henry's tradition had observed, "did not cool their respect to him." Barnes notes that he went "to the desert and lonely regions which surrounded the sea, where he might be in obscurity," but the people came after him from every quarter.

The verse thus holds together two movements that run through the chapter: the rulers withdrawing to plot his death, and the people pressing toward him for life. The same Lord whom the Pharisees and Herodians had just resolved to destroy is the Lord whom multitudes from the farthest borders seek out. Clarke draws the comfort: "if Christ be persecuted and abandoned by the wicked, there are a multitude of pious souls who earnestly seek and follow him" — for "he who labours for God will always find more than he loses, in the midst of all his contradictions and persecutions." His prudent retreat, far from hindering his work, became the occasion of its widest reach. Gill notes he went not from cowardice but "because his time was not yet come, and he had more work to do" — the same providence that ordered his hour also ordered the place where the next stage of his work would be done.
8:
"And from Jerusalem, and from Idumaea, and from beyond Jordan; and they about Tyre and Sidon, a great multitude, when they had heard what great things he did, came unto him." The witnesses dwell on the sweep of the geography and the breadth of his fame.

The crowd came, the commentators note, from every point of the compass — Galilee and Judea, Jerusalem "the place of wealth, and honour, and power" (Barnes), Idumaea to the south, "beyond Jordan" to the east, and "they about Tyre and Sidon" to the north-west (Robertson). Of Idumaea, named here alone in the New Testament, Barnes recalls it is "the land of Edom," the country of Esau (Genesis 25:30), whose people had spread into southern Judea and "submitted to the rite of circumcision." Ellicott notes it was then "under the government of Aretas" (2 Corinthians 11:32), whose intercourse with Galilee and Peraea had increased; and that the crowds "about Tyre and Sidon" explain how, later, our Lord's appearance in that region was welcomed (cf. Matthew 15:21).

Robertson observes that "many of these were probably Gentiles (Phoenicia and Decapolis) and may have known only the Greek language," so that "the fame of Jesus had spread through all the regions round about." Wesley adds that the Idumaeans "had now professed the Jewish religion above a hundred and fifty years."

The breadth of the gathering, the witnesses imply, is itself significant. Even as the leaders of his own nation conspire against him, the report of "what great things he did" draws seekers from the borders of the land and beyond — a foreshadowing, in the press of Jew and half-Gentile alike upon the Galilean shore, of the wider ingathering to come. Clarke's note on the previous verse applies here too: the persecution of the few could not stem the coming of the many, for the works of mercy preached more loudly than the malice of the rulers. Robertson adds that "there was a jam as the crowds came to Jesus by the Sea of Galilee," drawn from north and south and east alike — a single throng gathered out of peoples long divided by enmity and religion.
9:
"And he spake to his disciples, that a small ship should wait on him because of the multitude, lest they should throng him." The witnesses dwell on the boat, its purpose, and the pressure of the crowd.

The "small ship" is, more exactly, "a boat" (Barnes, Clarke), for "there were, properly speaking, no ships on the sea of Tiberias"; the word "ship," Clarke complains, "is utterly improper in many places of our translation, and tends to mislead the people" — "the lytil boot," as an Old English manuscript renders it. It "probably belonged to some of his disciples," who were fishermen, in which "he could sit, off from the shore, and teach the people, without being pressed by them" (Barnes).

Its purpose was to "wait on him." Robertson notes the boat "was to keep close (present tense subjunctive) to the shore in constant readiness and move as Jesus did," "at hand" whether needed or not. Ellicott observes that this incidental notice "shows that in what is recorded in Matthew 13:2" — where Jesus taught from a boat — "our Lord was but having recourse to a practice already familiar."

The reason was the throng. The boat was readied "lest they should throng him" — "press or crush him" (Robertson) — for the multitude pressed so eagerly that, Barnes notes, they "crowded on him, so as to endanger his life." Yet the provision was not flight: "Jesus stayed with the crowds, for they needed him" (Robertson). Henry's tradition reads in it a wise ordering of his work: the boat let him move easily "from place to place... without pressing through the crowds," so that, having done "the necessary business" in one place, "he might easily remove to another, where his presence was requisite." The Lord who could still a storm with a word yet used an ordinary fishing-boat for the ordinary management of his ministry, teaching, as the witnesses note, that prudence and power go together in his service. Gill notes that the order was given "in an authoritative way" to disciples who, being fishermen, "had fisher-boats at command," so that the means of his ministry were drawn from the ordinary callings of the men he had chosen.
10:
"For he had healed many; insomuch that they pressed upon him for to touch him, as many as had plagues." The witnesses dwell on the press of the sick and the meaning of "plagues."

The healing drew the crowd. "He had healed many" — "of various diseases," says Gill, "and the fame of this brought more still to him." The result was a dangerous crush. They "pressed upon him" (epipiptein), "were falling upon him to such an extent that it was dangerous" (Robertson); Clarke renders it "rushed upon him, through eagerness to have their... maladies immediately removed." They were "not hostile," Robertson adds, "but simply intensely eager, each to have his own case attended to," hoping "for a cure by contact with Christ" — "a really pathetic scene and a tremendous strain on Jesus."

"As many as had plagues" the commentators are careful to translate rightly. The word is "scourges" (mastigas) — "strokes," "the same word as in Mark 5:29" (Ellicott, Robertson) — and "plague," now confined to the pestilence, Barnes and Clarke warn, "does not express the meaning of the original, and tends to mislead." It means "diseases, or maladies of body or mind."

Wesley draws the deeper sense from the word: "scourges... seem to be those very painful or afflictive disorders which were frequently sent, or at least permitted, of God, as a scourge or punishment of sin." Henry's tradition reads the same lesson — "diseases are here called plagues, corrections, chastisements... designed to be, to make us smart for our sins, that thereby we may be made sorry for them" — and finds in the pressing crowd a parable of the soul: "this is the errand on which sickness is sent, to quicken us to enquire after Christ, and apply ourselves to him as our Physician." The sufferers who fought to touch him become, in the witnesses' reading, an image of the faith that, however weak, presses through every obstacle to the only Healer. Gill observes that the very multitude of cures became its own advertisement, for "the fame of this brought more still to him"; the mercy already shown was the surest preaching of the mercy yet to be sought.
11:
"And unclean spirits, when they saw him, fell down before him, and cried, saying, Thou art the Son of God." The witnesses dwell on the demons' confession, its repeated occurrence, and why it could not be received.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the recurring action. "Whensoever they beheld him" is an imperfect of "repeated action" (Robertson): "they kept falling down before him and crying," while Jesus "kept charging or rebuking them." The fall was no worship but dread — a deprecating of his wrath, as Henry's tradition put it, the devils "by their own terrors... compelled to own that he was the Son of God." The title, Barnes explains, is here "equivalent to the Messiah, who was among the Jews called the Son of God."

Barnes presses the demons' knowledge as proof of the unseen world. "This circumstance proves the existence of evil spirits": "if these were merely diseased or deranged persons, then it is strange that they should be endowed with knowledge so much superior to those in health"; but "if they were under the influence of an order of spirits superior to man," it is no wonder they knew the Messiah "even in the midst of his poverty" (cf. Mark 1:24; James 2:19). Clarke notes the textual variants on the confession's wording.

Ellicott explains why the cry was silenced. "It came in a form which our Lord could not receive": "the wild cry of the frenzied demoniac had no place in the evidence to which He appealed" (cf. John 5:31–37), and "tended, so far as it impressed men at all, to set them against the Teacher who was thus acknowledged." The witnesses thus draw a sober contrast: a truth confessed by demons, who "believe and tremble," yet are "excluded from having benefit by it" (Henry's tradition), while many men deny the very truth their salvation depends upon. The right confession from the wrong mouth, the commentators agree, Christ would not have — for testimony to him must come not from terror, but from faith. Clarke records that some manuscripts read the confession variously — "the Christ, the Son of God," or "the Holy One of God" — but all agree in the substance, that the powers of darkness knew and owned, against their will, the One whom the rulers of the people refused to acknowledge.
12:
"And he straitly charged them that they should not make him known." The witnesses dwell on the sternness of the command and the reasons for the silence.

The charge was severe. Gill renders it "vehemently rebuked them," or "threatened them much and vehemently" — "a strict and severe charge" laid both on "the devils that confessed him" and, some think, on "the many that were healed of their diseases." Barnes links it with the like commands elsewhere (Mark 1:25; Mark 1:34), where the unclean spirits are silenced as they recognise him.

Why silence a true confession? Wesley answers simply: "it was not the time; nor were they fit preachers." The witnesses give two grounds. First, the testimony came from an unworthy source — and, as Ellicott observed on the previous verse, such a cry "tended... to set men against the Teacher" rather than commend him; a confession wrung from terror is no fit herald of the gospel. Second, the time was not ripe: Barnes had noted (on v. 11) that Jesus "had not yet done what he wished to establish his claims to the Messiahship," and that a premature proclamation, while he was "poor and unhonoured," would have provoked the charge of imposture and endangered his life before "his hour was come."

The verse thus belongs to the larger pattern Mark records, in which Jesus repeatedly forbids the spreading of his fame and his Messianic title. The Lord who will at last command his disciples to preach him to all nations here muzzles even a true word about himself, because it springs from the wrong source and falls in the wrong season. The economy of his ministry — and above all of his passion — was not to be hurried by the clamour of demons, but to unfold in the time and by the means the Father had appointed; and so, the witnesses note, he leaves "his own works to praise him," refusing testimony that faith had not prompted and that the hour did not yet allow. Henry's tradition draws the lesson for us: when we do what is praiseworthy and yet covet not the praise of men for it, "then the same mind is in us which was in Christ Jesus," who would let his own works, and not the cries of demons, declare him.
13:
"And he goeth up into a mountain, and calleth unto him whom he would: and they came unto him." The witnesses dwell on the prayer that preceded the choice, the sovereignty of the call, and its symbolism.

The mountain was a place of prayer. Luke adds that Jesus "went up to pray" and "continued all night in prayer to God" (cf. Luke 6:12), and Gill, Henry's tradition, and Ellicott all note it. Henry draws the rule: "ministers must be set apart with solemn prayer," and "though Christ had authority to confer the gifts of the Holy Ghost, yet, to set us an example, he prayed." Calvin makes it "a perpetual rule, to begin with prayer, when we are about to choose pastors to churches; otherwise, what we attempt will not succeed well."

The call was wholly free. "He called unto him whom he would" — "not such as we should have thought fittest," says Henry's tradition, "but such as he thought fit to call, and determined to make fit"; Robertson notes the emphatic "himself" — those "whom he really wished to be with him." Wesley adds that "his grace is his own." And it was effectual: "they came unto him," for "all that the Father gave him shall come to him" (John 6:37; John 17:6).

The fathers read the scene mystically. Bede notes that "it was not a matter of their choice and zeal, but of Divine condescension and grace, that they should be called to the Apostleship," and that "the mount... shows the lofty righteousness in which they were to be instructed": the twelve answer to the tribes encamped about the tabernacle, "three times four," sent "through the four quarters of the whole world" to baptize the nations. Calvin observes that this was election "to enjoy his private instructions with a view to the apostleship," not yet their sending forth (which Matthew records); their heavenly Master "chose to train and accustom them gradually to so arduous an employment." Calvin adds that the choice rested "to the unmixed grace of Christ, and not to any excellence of their own," for if the more excellent had been chosen, "this will not apply to Judas"; the apostleship was bestowed "by the free mercy of God" on the unworthy.
14:
"And he ordained twelve, that they should be with him, and that he might send them forth to preach." The witnesses examine the "ordaining," the number, and the two purposes of the call.

The word "ordained," the commentators caution, does not carry the later sense. It means "he made" or "appointed" — "he made twelve" (Clarke), "appointed twelve to be with him" (Barnes); Robertson notes the better-attested reading "whom he also named apostles" (so Luke 6:13), Jesus himself giving "the name apostle or missionary (from apostellō, to send) to this group."

The number, the witnesses agree, was chosen with meaning. It answered "the number of the twelve tribes of Israel" (Henry's tradition, Barnes, Robertson), the twelve being destined as "princes in the new Israel" (Robertson; cf. Matthew 19:28; Revelation 21:14). Barnes adds a practical reason: so many "could not be supposed to be imposed upon," competent witnesses "destined to go into all the world."

The two purposes Mark names the witnesses make central. They were "that they should be with him, and that he might send them forth." "They were not ready to be sent forth," says Robertson, "till they had been with Jesus for some time"; "this is one of the chief tasks of Christ, to train this group of men." Henry's tradition presses the same: they "must be with him constantly, to be witnesses of his doctrine, manner of life, and patience... and especially that they might attest the truth of his miracles," for "Christ's ministers must be much with him." Only then are they "sent forth to preach: not to preach till they were sent, and not to be sent till by a long and intimate acquaintance with Christ they were fitted." The double ministry — to preach and to cast out demons (v. 15) — would mark their work, the witnesses note, though, as Robertson observes, "one does not necessarily involve the other." Henry's tradition notes that "it would require time to fit them for that which he designed them for," so that the long fellowship with Christ was itself their training for the work to come.
15:
"And to have power to heal sicknesses, and to cast out devils." The witnesses dwell on the threefold work conferred and on what the gift reveals of Christ.

The power given the commentators read as the equipment of the apostolic ministry. Clarke lays out "the business of a minister of Christ" in three parts: "to preach the Gospel," "to be the physician of souls," and "to wage war with the devil, and destroy his kingdom." Gill takes the "sicknesses" as "all manner of corporeal diseases that attend men and women," and Robertson (on the previous verse) had distinguished "this double ministry of preaching and healing" — the two being "different," so that "one does not necessarily involve the other."

The conferring of the power, Henry's tradition argues, "put a very great honour upon them, beyond that of the great men of the earth," and proves something of Christ himself. "This showed that the power which Christ had to work these miracles was an original power; that he had it not as a Servant, but as a Son in his own house, in that he could confer it upon others, and invest them with it." Henry cites the legal maxim, "Deputatus non potest deputare — he that is only deputed himself cannot depute another"; but "our Lord Jesus had life in himself, and the Spirit without measure," and "could give this power even to the weak and foolish things of the world."

The witnesses thus find in this brief clause both the pattern of the church's work and a quiet testimony to the deity of Christ. The miracles of healing and exorcism were to "bear witness," as Bede put it, "to the greatness of their heavenly promises," so that "they who preached unheard-of things might do unheard-of actions." The same power that flowed from Christ at a word he now lodges in the hands of twelve unlearned men — a delegation possible, the commentators agree, only to one who held that power not on loan but as his own. Henry's tradition presses the honour conferred: the gift set the apostles "beyond... the great men of the earth," for to heal the body and to vanquish the devil are works that publish the kingdom more loudly than any earthly authority.
16:
"And Simon he surnamed Peter." The witnesses dwell on the new name, when it was given, and what it signified.

The name, all agree, means rock or stone. "Cephas" in Hebrew, "Peter" in Greek and Latin (Bede), it was given, Gill says, "because of his courage and constancy, his strength and fortitude, steadiness and firmness of mind." Robertson notes the Greek idiom — Jesus "gave this surname in addition to Simon" — and refers it to the first meeting (John 1:42). Augustine guards the chronology: "let no one suppose that Simon now received his name," for John records it was said long before; "Mark gives this account by way of recapitulation," noting briefly "that the Lord gave him that name."

Calvin, for the Reformed, weighs its meaning. "Though all Christians must be living stones of the spiritual temple, yet Christ gave this name peculiarly to Simon, according to the measure of grace which he intended to bestow." This "is not inconsistent with the shameful weakness which he manifested in denying his Lord," for the title "showed his invincible power and steadiness, which continued till his death." But, Calvin adds, "it is absurd in the Papists to infer from this that the Church is founded on him."

Bede draws the deeper reference. "Peter... is drawn from a stone"; and "this rock," he says with Paul, "was Christ" (1 Corinthians 10:4): "as Christ was the true light, and allowed also that the Apostles should be called the light of the world, so also to Simon, who believed on the rock Christ, he gave the name of Rock." Theophylact adds that Christ reckoned Simon among the apostles though his fall was future, "that we may learn that God does not repel any man for wickedness which is future, but counts him worthy on account of his present virtue." The new name, the witnesses conclude, was both a promise and a calling — a steadiness derived not from Simon himself but from the Rock on whom he believed. Gill notes the name was given "when Christ first called him to be his disciple and apostle," and renewed at his great confession; it marked from the outset the man Christ would make him, not the man he then was.
17:
"And James the son of Zebedee, and John the brother of James; and he surnamed them Boanerges, which is, The sons of thunder." The witnesses dwell on the strange name and its meaning.

The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the word. It is "an Aramaic compound, B'nè-regesh, sons of thunder" (Ellicott), though, Robertson notes, "the reason for it is not clear." Lightfoot and Clarke discuss the philology at length — the difficulty of the Hebrew Scheva pronounced "oa," Clarke proposing beney raam or beni reges — Lightfoot defending Mark's spelling against Beza, since "among the Jews the pronouncing of some letters... was so different and indifferent" from region to region.

On the sense the witnesses largely agree it marks a fiery zeal. Ellicott sees "a witness to the fiery zeal of the sons of Zebedee," shown in their wish "to call down fire from heaven on the Samaritans" (Luke 9:54), John's stopping of one who cast out devils, and the brothers' request to sit at the right hand and the left (Matthew 20:21) — "that burning zeal that made James the proto-martyr" (Acts 12:2). Barnes and Robertson concur, while Henry's tradition and Wesley read it more of "the zeal and fervency of their spirits" and "their fervent manner of preaching," "the power of their word."

Calvin, for the Reformed, hears in it a commission of utterance: Christ "called the sons of Zebedee" thus "because he was to give them a powerful voice, that they might thunder throughout the whole world in preaching" — "and that thunder is heard, in the present day, from the mouth of John." Ellicott traces the same temper forward into "the multiplied thunderings and voices of the Apocalypse" (Revelation 4:5; 8:5). The name, the witnesses conclude, marked not a fault to be condemned but a force to be sanctified — a zeal that, once tempered by grace, would shake the earth for the gospel. Yet John, Henry's tradition observes, "one of those sons of thunder, was full of love and tenderness, as appears by his epistles, and was the beloved disciple" — proof that the same fervency which thundered against sin could melt into the gentlest charity.
18:
"And Andrew, and Philip, and Bartholomew, and Matthew, and Thomas, and James the son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus, and Simon the Canaanite." The witnesses identify the names and draw out their meanings.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the identifications. Andrew (from anēr, a man) and Philip are Greek names (Robertson); Bartholomew, "son of Tolmai," is "the Nathanael of John's Gospel" (John 21:2), who "probably had both names"; Matthew, the former publican, is a Hebrew name meaning "gift of God"; Thomas is "Twin" (Didymus); there are two named James, this one "the son of Alphaeus." Thaddaeus, Bede notes, "is the same person as Luke calls... Jude of James, for he was the brother of James." Of "Simon the Canaanite," Ellicott corrects the rendering: better "Cananaean," "the Aramaic equivalent of Zelotes" — the Zealot.

Pseudo-Jerome reads each name as a mystery. Bartholomew means "the son of him who suspends the waters"; Matthew, "given," "to whom it is given... not only to obtain remission of sins, but to be enrolled in the number of the Apostles"; Thomas, "abyss," for "men who have knowledge by the power of God put forward many deep things"; James the son of Alphaeus, "of the learned," whose wrestling "is not against flesh and blood, but against spiritual wickedness"; Thaddaeus, "he who guards the heart... in all watchfulness"; and Simon, "laying aside sorrow," called Zealot "because the zeal of the Lord ate him up."

The witnesses thus present the roll not as a bare list but as a gallery of grace. Drawn from differing callings and temperaments — fishermen and a publican, a zealot and men otherwise unknown — they are bound together only by the call of Christ. The very obscurity of several, the commentators imply, magnifies the choice: that the kingdom should be built by such men, named here once and for all, is itself a token that "God has chosen the weak things of the world." Each name, whether traced to its Greek, Hebrew, or Aramaic root, becomes for the fathers a small parable of the character grace would form in its bearer. Bede adds that Andrew, a Greek name, means "manly," "for he manfully adhered to the Lord" — the first-called brother of Peter standing, in the fathers' reading, for the courage that cleaves to Christ.
19:
"And Judas Iscariot, which also betrayed him." The witnesses dwell on the traitor named last, the meaning of his name, and the mystery of his choice.

He is placed last, Gill notes, "to distinguish him from the other Judas" and for "the following reason" — his treachery, which the Evangelist at once records. The surname the fathers trace to his origin: "Judas, Iscariot, from the village from which he had his origin, or... from the tribe of Issachar" (Bede). Pseudo-Jerome reads the name darkly — "Judas means boaster, or vain-glorious; and Iscariot, the memory of death" — and likens him to "the proud and vain-glorious confessors in the Church, as Simon Magus, and Arius."

The great difficulty, Calvin faces squarely: "why did our Lord deliberately choose Judas, who, he perfectly knew, was unworthy of the honour, and would be his betrayer?" He answers that "our Lord expressly intended to prevent future offences, that we may not feel excessive uneasiness when unprincipled men occupy the situation of teachers in the Church, or when professors of the Gospel become apostates." In Judas he "gave... an instance of fearful defection," that "those who occupy a higher rank may not indulge in self-complacency." God, "putting a devil into the company of eleven angels," so "guided the result, that the fall of this man, instead of shaking, has rather confirmed, the faith of his Church." The stability of the Church, Calvin insists, must not "depend on men."

Theophylact adds the consoling counterpart: Christ reckoned even Simon among the apostles though his fall was future, "that we may learn that God does not repel any man for wickedness which is future, but counts him worthy on account of his present virtue." So the roll of the Twelve closes, the witnesses observe, on a solemn note — eleven who would stand and one who would fall — teaching the Church neither to presume on office nor to stumble when an office-bearer betrays his Lord. The honour, Calvin concludes, was given that no one "may abuse the honour which God has conferred upon him, and likewise that, when even the pillars fall, those who appear to be the weakest of believers may remain steady."
20:
"And the multitude cometh together again, so that they could not so much as eat bread." The witnesses dwell on the press of the crowd and the consuming demands of the work.

The crowd reassembled at once. Gill notes that those who had thronged him by the sea, learning he had returned "to Capernaum, and was at Simon's house, flocked thither in great numbers"; Robertson pictures the jam "indoors with the great crowd in the house and at the door," so great that "they could not rest, could not eat, and apparently Jesus could not even teach." Ellicott calls the touch "eminently characteristic of St. Mark," "as if springing from actual reminiscence of that crowded scene."

That "they could not so much as eat bread" the commentators read as the cost of zeal. Barnes draws the lesson that "the affairs of religion may so occupy the attention of ministers... as to... prevent their engaging in their customary pursuits," religion being "far more important than the ordinary business of this life"; yet he adds that ordinarily it "is consistent with a close attention to worldly business," as in "the illustrious example... of our Saviour himself." Henry's tradition had read the same: those "whose hearts are enlarged in the work of God can easily bear with great inconveniences," and "will rather lose a meal's meat at any time than slip an opportunity of doing good."

Calvin, for the Reformed, uses the verse as the hinge into what follows. The Evangelist "takes in a somewhat extended period of time, when he passes from the miracles to that wicked conspiracy which the relatives of Christ formed." It is "wonderful that such wickedness should have been found among the relatives of Christ, who ought to have been the first to aid him." The very throng that pressed for life around the Saviour becomes the occasion for his own kin to judge him mad — a contrast, the witnesses observe, between the hunger of the multitude for his word and the blindness of those nearest to him in the flesh. Robertson notes the crowd "reassembled at once on Christ's return from the mountain," so that the strain was unbroken — the same scene of pressing need that Mark records again at 1:32 and 2:2.
21:
"And when his friends heard of it, they went out to lay hold on him: for they said, He is beside himself." The witnesses identify the "friends," the charge, and what it reveals.

The "friends" are, more exactly, the kinsfolk. The phrase (hoi par' autou), Robertson explains, "most likely means the kinspeople or family of Jesus," confirmed by "his mother and his brothers" in v.31; Ellicott reads "those from His home." They "went out to lay hold on him" — "to take him away from the multitude, and to remove him to his home" (Barnes), "forcibly if need be" (Robertson), supposing him overwrought.

"He is beside himself" (exestē) the commentators take as a charge of derangement. Barnes notes the report likely "raised by his enemies" and believed by relatives who "did not believe on him" (cf. John 7:20); the same charge was later urged (John 10:20, "He hath a devil, and is mad"), and brought even against Paul (2 Corinthians 5:13). Lightfoot adds, from the rabbinic idiom, that the phrase "does not always signify madness... but sometimes... some discomposure of the understanding for the present, from some too vehement passion." Robertson judges that Mary "probably felt that Jesus was overwrought," wishing "to take him home... that he might get rest."

Barnes turns it to a lasting observation: "the charge of derangement on account of attention to religion has not been confined to our Saviour." Let a man "spend much of his time in prayer, and have no relish for the ordinary amusements... and the world is not slow to call it derangement" (cf. Hosea 9:7); yet men may "plunge into the vortex of fashion, and folly, and vice... and in the view of the world it is wisdom." Calvin draws the deeper lesson: "the light of faith does not proceed from flesh and blood, but from heavenly grace" — for if even Christ's intimates could not perceive the power of the Spirit shining in him, no man may "glory in any thing else than in the regeneration of the Spirit."
22:
"And the scribes which came down from Jerusalem said, He hath Beelzebub, and by the prince of the devils casteth he out devils." The witnesses dwell on the source of the slander and its desperate logic.

The scribes came with intent. Henry's tradition notes they "came down from Jerusalem... on purpose to hinder the progress of the doctrine of Christ," and that, as "the most polite and learned scribes," their "reputation... would have an influence" on the country folk, who "had never thought of this base suggestion... till the scribes from Jerusalem put it into their heads." Gill calls them "letter learned, and artful, and cunning."

Their charge was a forced evasion. They "could not deny but that he cast out devils, which plainly bespoke him sent of God," says Henry's tradition; "but they insinuated that he had Beelzebub on his side... and by the prince of the devils cast out devils" — "Satan is not cast out, he only goes out by consent." The name Beelzebub, the fathers note (Bede), means "lord of flies," the god of Ekron, taken as "prince of the devils." It was, Barnes observes, "on the occasion of his healing a man possessed with a devil" that the scribes "charged it on a compact... between him and the prince of the devils" (cf. Matthew 12:24).

Wesley draws the sobering lesson on learning misused: "how easily may a man of learning elude the strongest proof of a work of God! How readily can he account for every incident without ever taking God into the question." The same miracles that should have led the scribes to faith they twisted into a charge of diabolic league — preferring, the witnesses note, the most monstrous explanation to the plain confession that the finger of God was upon him. Their learning, far from opening their eyes, became the instrument by which they "drank in the poison" and spread it to the people. Gill notes they "came down from Jerusalem... into Galilee" on purpose, men "in their way, letter learned, and artful, and cunning," who sought to "take every advantage they could against him" — travelling far to do mischief that the country scribes had never devised.
23:
"And he called them unto him, and said unto them in parables, How can Satan cast out Satan?" The witnesses dwell on Christ's gracious method and the absurdity he exposes.

He "called them unto him," Henry's tradition notes, "as one desirous they should be convinced," treating them "with all the freedom, friendliness, and familiarity that could be," reasoning the case "that every mouth may be stopped." The "parables," Ellicott explains, use the word "in its wider sense, as including any form of argument from analogy"; Robertson calls them "crisp pungent thrusts that exposed the inconsistencies of the scribes."

The question "How can Satan cast out Satan?" lays bare the self-contradiction of their charge. Pseudo-Chrysostom states the logic: "a kingdom divided against itself by civil war must be desolated, which is exemplified both in a house and in a city. Wherefore also if Satan's kingdom be divided against itself, so that Satan expels Satan from men, the desolation of the kingdom of the devils is at hand." For "their kingdom consists in keeping men under their dominion"; "if therefore they are driven away from men, it amounts to nothing less than the dissolution of their kingdom."

The witnesses thus find the answer at once disarming and decisive. It was plain, says Henry's tradition, that "the doctrine of Christ made war upon the devil's kingdom," and that "the casting of him out of the bodies of people confirmed that doctrine"; therefore "it cannot be imagined that he should come into such a design, for every one knows that Satan is no fool, nor will act so directly against his own interest." Robertson notes the verb for Satan's "casting out" is the very word used of casting out demons — so that, on the scribes' theory, Satan would be expelling himself. By a single question, framed as a homely comparison, Christ turns their slander into an absurdity, and prepares the way for the parable of the strong man bound. The Gloss observes that, having shown by analogy "that a devil cannot cast out a devil," the Lord goes on to show how he can be expelled — namely, by one stronger entering to bind him, which is the burden of the verse that follows.
24:
"And if a kingdom be divided against itself, that kingdom cannot stand." The witnesses read this as the first step of Christ's argument against the Beelzebub charge — the universal law that internal division destroys.

Gill takes the clause to apply both to "any of the kingdoms of this world" and, by extension, to "the kingdom of Satan." The principle is one no one could dispute: as Pseudo-Chrysostom states it, "a kingdom divided against itself by civil war must be desolated"; civil war within a realm is the surest road to its ruin. Robertson notes the precise word ("divided," meristhē, parted into portions): a kingdom broken into factions warring on each other cannot endure, for its strength lay in its unity.

The force of the saying lies in its self-evidence. Christ does not here assert anything peculiar to himself or his cause; he appeals to a truth written into the order of every human society. The greatest empire, once its powers turn upon one another, totters and falls; history furnishes no exception. By beginning with so plain a maxim, the witnesses observe, the Lord secures the assent of his hearers before he applies it — they cannot deny the principle without denying the evidence of their own eyes.

And the application, though not yet spoken, is already implied. If a kingdom cannot stand when divided, then Satan's kingdom — were he indeed casting out Satan — could not stand either. The scribes had charged that the prince of devils was at war with his own servants; but such a war, Christ shows, would be the kingdom of darkness signing its own ruin. Henry's tradition draws the conclusion the verse points toward: "Satan is no fool, nor will act so directly against his own interest." The first link in the chain of reasoning is thus a truth so obvious that, once granted, it leaves the accusers no escape from the absurdity of their own charge. The kingdom in view, Gill notes, may be read of "any of the kingdoms of this world" and equally "the kingdom of Satan"; the maxim holds at every level, so that what dooms an earthly realm dooms the realm of darkness too, if once it be turned against itself.
25:
"And if a house be divided against itself, that house cannot stand." The witnesses read this as the second step of the argument, narrowing the universal law from the kingdom to the household.

Gill takes "house" to mean "any family, small or great." The move from kingdom to house, the commentators note, presses the same truth nearer home: as Pseudo-Chrysostom said, the desolation of a divided realm "is exemplified both in a house and in a city." What is true of the largest dominion is true of the smallest society — the family. Robertson again marks the word "divided" (meristhē): a household whose members are set against one another loses the bond that held it, and falls.

The two illustrations together — kingdom and house — cover the whole range of human association, from the greatest to the least, so that the principle admits of no exception. A divided empire crumbles; a divided family disintegrates; division is, everywhere and always, the seed of ruin. By doubling the example, Christ leaves the scribes no room to plead a special case.

The application to Satan's kingdom grows the more inescapable. If even a single house cannot survive its own internal strife, how could the whole organised power of darkness endure if its prince were turning against his own? The charge that Christ cast out devils "by the prince of the devils" would require that Satan's house be at war with itself — and such a house, by Christ's own homely logic, "cannot stand." The witnesses thus see in the saying not merely a refutation but a warning written into the nature of things: division destroys whatever it enters, whether a kingdom, a family, or — as the next verse will show — the kingdom of evil itself. The same law that condemns the scribes' slander also commends to every society the unity without which nothing endures. Robertson notes the conditions are stated as real possibilities held up for judgment, not idle suppositions; Christ invites the scribes themselves to weigh whether the kingdom of evil could really be supposed to be at war within itself, and so to feel the weight of their own contradiction.
26:
"And if Satan rise up against himself, and be divided, he cannot stand, but hath an end." The witnesses read this as the conclusion of the argument, applying the law of division directly to the devil.

Gill states the case plainly: Satan must "rise up against himself" — "as he must do in such a case as this, if devils are cast out by Beelzebub, the prince of devils." The two premises now meet their point: if no kingdom and no house can stand when divided, neither can Satan. Were he expelling his own servants, his power would be at civil war with itself, and the inevitable result would be not merely weakness but ruin — "he cannot stand, but hath an end."

Pseudo-Chrysostom had already drawn out the force: since "their kingdom consists in keeping men under their dominion," to drive devils from men "amounts to nothing less than the dissolution of their kingdom." The scribes' theory, pressed to its conclusion, would mean that Satan was busy destroying himself — a supposition Henry's tradition dismisses as plainly false: "Satan is no fool, nor will act so directly against his own interest." Robertson notes the grim finality of the phrase "hath an end" (telos echei): the divided kingdom of evil would simply terminate.

The witnesses thus see the argument complete. Christ has met the slander not with indignation but with irresistible logic, turning the scribes' own words into an absurdity. Either his casting out of devils proves the kingdom of darkness is being invaded by a stronger power — which is the truth — or it proves that Satan is suicidally divided, which no one believes. There is no third possibility. And the deeper truth lurks beneath the logic: the very fact that the demons flee at Christ's word shows that the strong man is not at war with himself but is being bound by another — the theme the next verse takes up. Satan's house is not collapsing from within; it is being plundered from without. Gill observes that, on the scribes' own premise, Satan "must" rise against himself "in such a case as this, if devils are cast out by Beelzebub"; the charge cannot be held without supposing the devil bent on his own destruction — a thing, the witnesses agree, that no one in his senses believes of so cunning an adversary.
27:
"No man can enter into a strong man's house, and spoil his goods, except he will first bind the strong man; and then he will spoil his house." The witnesses read the parable as the true account of the exorcisms: not collusion with Satan, but conquest of him.

The figure the commentators interpret with one voice. The "strong man" is Satan; "his goods" are "the men into whom he is received" (Theophylact); to "spoil his house" is to free those he has possessed. "Unless therefore a man first conquers the devil," Theophylact asks, "how can he deprive him of his goods?" — so Christ, in casting out demons, "first spoils the devils and vanquishes them, and is their enemy." Robertson makes the point sharp: the verb (diarpasai) is to "plunder, thoroughly ransack" — "a picture of Satan plundering the demons" reversed, for "Jesus is the conqueror of Satan, not in league with him" (cf. Matthew 12:29; Isaiah 49:24).

Bede draws out the breadth of the conquest. "The Lord has bound the strong man, that is, the devil," restraining him "from seducing the elect," and "has spoiled his house, and his goods, that is men, because he has snatched them from the snares of the devil, and has united them to his Church"; the "four parts of the world, over which the old enemy had sway," he has "distributed to the Apostles and their successors, that they may convert the people to the way of life." Cyril of Jerusalem adds that none could "take the body which is another's and cast it into hell, except he first bind the strong man, and spoil his goods."

The witnesses thus find in the parable the decisive answer to the slander. The scribes had said Christ was Satan's ally; the very deliverance of the possessed proves the opposite — that he has invaded the strong man's house and bound him. Henry's tradition reads it as the pattern of all Christ's work: "his design is to enter into the strong man's house, to take possession of the interest he has in the world, and to spoil his goods, and convert them to his own service." Every soul freed from bondage is a token that the strong man is bound and his house being plundered by a stronger.
28:
"Verily I say unto you, All sins shall be forgiven unto the sons of men, and blasphemies wherewith soever they shall blaspheme." The witnesses dwell on the vast breadth of the forgiveness here promised, against which the coming exception will stand out the more sharply.

The promise is sweeping. Henry's tradition marks it: "the gospel promiseth, because Christ hath purchased, forgiveness for the greatest sins and sinners"; even "many of those who reviled Christ on the cross — which was a blaspheming of the Son of man, aggravated to the highest degree — found mercy," and "Christ himself prayed, Father, forgive them" (cf. Luke 23:34). Gill notes the words are addressed to "the Scribes and Pharisees, who had not only blasphemed him, but the Spirit of God also" — so that the warning is set within an offer of pardon. Barnes refers it to the parallels (Matthew 12:31; Luke 12:10).

Bede guards the promise on two sides. "All sins and blasphemies are not indeed remitted to all men, but to those who have gone through a repentance in this life sufficient for their sins." So, he says, neither is Novatus right, "who denied that any pardon should be granted to penitents who had lapsed in time of martyrdom," nor Origen, "who asserts that after the general judgment... all sinners will receive pardon" — the one too narrow, the other too broad. The forgiveness is full and free, but it is for the penitent.

The witnesses thus set the verse as a deliberate foil. Before naming the one sin that "hath never forgiveness," Christ first proclaims that there is forgiveness for all the rest — for every sin and every blasphemy, however gross, of those who turn. The exception that follows is therefore no narrowing of grace, but the marking of a single, dreadful self-exclusion from a mercy otherwise without limit. The same lips that pronounce the unpardonable sin first fling open the door of pardon as wide as human guilt can reach. Gill stresses that the word is spoken to the very Scribes and Pharisees "who had not only blasphemed him, but the Spirit of God also" — so that even these, the warning notwithstanding, are not shut out from the offer, if only they will not persist in naming the Spirit's work the devil's.
29:
"But he that shall blaspheme against the Holy Ghost hath never forgiveness, but is in danger of eternal damnation." The witnesses define the unpardonable sin, explain why it is unpardonable, and weigh the closing word.

The sin, the commentators agree, is the deliberate ascribing of the Spirit's works to the devil. Gill defines it as blaspheming "against his person, and the works performed by him, by ascribing them to diabolical power and influence, as the Scribes did"; Robertson: "they had attributed to the devil the work of the Holy Spirit," and "this is the unpardonable sin, and it can be committed today by men who call the work of Christ the work of the devil."

Why it has no forgiveness the witnesses explain. It is "blasphemy against God," for "the operation of the Holy Ghost is the kingdom of God" (Pseudo-Chrysostom). Henry's tradition reasons that it is "a sin against the last remedy": by setting aside "such a strong conviction with such a weak evasion," and outfacing "all the gifts of the Holy Ghost after Christ's ascension," the blasphemer leaves nothing that could "bring them to repentance." Augustine reads it more inwardly as "impenitence itself... which hath no remission," the treasuring up of "an impenitent heart"; Pseudo-Jerome, as the state of one who, "understanding who Christ was, declared that He was the prince of the devils." Bede adds the necessary limit: those "who do not believe the Holy Spirit to be God" through "human ignorance, not... devilish malice" are not guilty of this unpardonable blasphemy.

The closing phrase the grammatical commentators refine. Ellicott and Robertson prefer the better-attested reading "guilty of an eternal sin" (aiōniou hamartēmatos) rather than "eternal damnation" — "one which will, with its consequences, extend throughout the ages" (cf. Matthew 12:32; Hebrews 10:29). The witnesses thus hold the warning in its full gravity while guarding the trembling conscience: it is not weakness of feeling, nor doubt, nor even reviling spoken in ignorance, but the settled, clear-eyed malice that names the Spirit's work the devil's — and so bars itself from the only door of repentance.
30:
"Because they said, He hath an unclean spirit." The witnesses note that this closing word, peculiar to Mark, fixes the meaning of the whole warning.

Gill makes it the key: the Lord spoke of the unpardonable sin "because they had been guilty of it." "They charged Christ with having a devil, and his miracles with being wrought by the help of the devil," when "they knew in their own consciences they were works... wrought by the finger and Spirit of God." It is this — the knowing attribution of the Spirit's work to an unclean spirit — that "is mentioned as a reason why our Lord said what he did concerning that sin."

Ellicott notes the clause is "peculiar to St. Mark," "as though he would explain to his readers what it was that had called forth so awful a warning." He adds a careful qualification: Mark "does not absolutely identify what had been said with the sin against the Holy Ghost, but it tended to that sin, and therefore made the warning necessary." The scribes' words were not, perhaps, the completed sin, but they were its very form and direction; and Christ's warning was meant to arrest them before the tendency hardened into the final, irrevocable malice.

Wesley finds in the verse the plainest possible definition. "Is it not astonishing that men who have ever read these words should doubt what is the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost? Can any words declare more plainly that it is the ascribing those miracles to the power of the devil which Christ wrought by the power of the Holy Ghost?" The witnesses thus close the passage by anchoring its terror to a concrete act: not a mysterious or unknowable offence to torment the anxious, but the specific, deliberate slander the scribes had just uttered. The Evangelist's added line, they agree, is at once a definition and a mercy — telling us exactly what the dreadful sin is, so that those who fear they have committed it, simply by fearing, show that they have not. Gill adds that the scribes spoke "against their own consciences," knowing the works to be wrought "by the finger and Spirit of God"; it was this knowing contradiction, not any mere error of judgement, that made their words so perilous and drew from Christ so solemn a warning.
31:
"There came then his brethren and his mother, and, standing without, sent unto him, calling him." The witnesses dwell on the kinsfolk's errand, the identity of the "brethren," and the deeper meaning of their standing without.

These are the relatives of v.21, now arrived. Theophylact reads the mother's coming with sympathy — "urged by the sympathy of her love" — and insists the scene "does not deny His mother," but honours her "on account of her possessing every other virtue." Robertson paints "the pathetic picture of the mother and brothers standing on the outside of the house," "crowded out," sending word through the throng "calling him." Wesley notes the brethren are "named first, as being first and most earnest in the design of taking him: for neither did these of his brethren believe on him" (cf. John 7:5).

On the "brethren" Bede states the church's mind: they "must not be thought to be the sons of the ever-virgin Mary, as Helvidius says, nor the sons of Joseph by a former marriage, as some think, but rather... His relations." Henry's tradition reads their conduct as "disrespect," sending "in a message to call him out... as if he must leave his work."

The fathers add a mystical layer. For Bede, "the mother and brother of Jesus" signify "the synagogue... and the Jewish people, who, while the Saviour is teaching within, come to Him, and are not able to enter, because they cannot understand spiritual things"; "the crowd eagerly enter, because when the Jews delayed, the Gentiles flocked to Christ." The kindred "who stand without" are "the Jews who obstinately remained without, guarding the letter," wishing to "compel the Lord to go forth to them to teach carnal things." "If therefore not even His parents when standing without are acknowledged, how shall we be acknowledged, if we stand without? For the word is within, and the light within." Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that another Evangelist records the brethren "did not believe on Him," which agrees with their seeking him from without; and Henry’s tradition notes the cruelty of interrupting his work "under pretence of his sparing himself," when he "preferred the salvation of souls before his own life."
32:
"And the multitude sat about him, and they said unto him, Behold, thy mother and thy brethren without seek for thee." The witnesses dwell on the scene and the message relayed through the crowd.

The multitude, Robertson notes, "sat in a circle (kuklō) around Jesus, with the disciples forming a sort of inner circle." Gill explains that, the people being seated "all around him," "there was no such thing as the messenger coming near him; but the message being whispered from one to another, it came to those who sat nearest him," and so was passed to Jesus. The errand of the kinsfolk had to travel inward through the very throng that had displaced them.

Ellicott adds a textual note of some weight: "many MSS. of high authority add, 'and thy sisters,'" which "explains the emphatic addition of that word in v.35." If the sisters were indeed among those seeking him without, then his answer — naming "brother, and sister, and mother" — answers point for point the family circle that stood outside.

The witnesses find the scene quietly significant. Within the house is a circle gathered to hear the word; without stands the natural family, "seeking" him but not entering. The contrast that runs through the whole passage — between those near in the flesh and those near in faith — is here set in a single picture: the seated hearers who "sat about him" and the kinsfolk who "stand without." Theophylact's principle, stated on the previous verse, governs the moment: by what follows "the Lord shows that we should honour those who are relations by faith rather than those who are relations by blood." The message delivered, the stage is set for the saying that will redraw the bonds of kinship around obedience to God. Gill notes the multitude "sat about him... to hear him preach," so that the kinsfolk's claim of blood had to make its way through a company gathered by the claim of the word; the very difficulty of the message reaching him pictures how far the natural tie now stood from the spiritual fellowship that pressed close around the Teacher.
33:
"And he answered them, saying, Who is my mother, or my brethren?" The witnesses are careful to read the question rightly — not as a rebuff of his mother, but as the lifting of kinship to a higher plane.

The fathers guard against misreading it as cold. Pseudo-Chrysostom insists "He does not here mention His mother and His brethren altogether with reproof, but to shew that a man must honour his own soul above all earthly kindred." The question was fitly put "to those who called Him to speak with His mother and relations, as if it were a more useful task than the teaching of salvation."

Bede draws the same balance. "Being asked... by a message to go out, He declines, not as though He refused the dutiful service of His mother, but to shew that He owes more to His Father's mysteries than to His mother's feelings." "Nor does He rudely despise His brothers, but, preferring His spiritual work to fleshly relationship, He teaches us that religion is the bond of the heart rather than that of the body." Gill notes the answer is addressed to "the multitude that sat about him, and informed him of his mother and brethren being without, and desirous of speaking with him."

The witnesses thus find in the question no slight upon natural affection, but a deliberate ranking of loyalties. Christ was engaged in "the teaching of salvation," and to break it off at the call of kindred would have been to prefer the lesser bond to the greater. The question, far from dishonouring Mary, exalts a relationship open to all — the kinship of those who do the will of God. By asking "Who is my mother, or my brethren?" he prepares his hearers to receive the startling answer that the truest family of Jesus is not bounded by blood but constituted by faith and obedience, so that the humblest believer in the circle around him stands nearer to him than the natural ties that waited without. Clarke, referring to the parallel in Matthew, notes that the same lesson is taught there: Christ owns no kinship so dear as that of doing the will of God, and will not let even the dearest earthly claim interrupt the work of salvation he was sent to do.
34:
"And he looked round about on them which sat about him, and said, Behold my mother and my brethren!" The witnesses dwell on the gesture, the honour conferred on the disciples, and the guard against excessive devotion to Mary.

The look is another of Mark's vivid touches. Ellicott renders it "looking round on those who sat in a circle round Him"; Robertson calls it "another of Mark's life-like touches," and Gill says he looked round "to find out his disciples among them, and point them out particularly, by stretching forth his hand towards them." Wesley adds that he spoke "with the utmost sweetness."

The saying confers an astonishing honour. Chrysostom draws the lesson that "we should honour those who are relations by faith rather than those who are relations by blood"; indeed "a man is made the mother of Jesus by preaching Him, for he, as it were, brings forth the Lord when he pours Him into the heart of his hearers." Henry's tradition reads it as a "superlative honour" set on "ordinary believers," who are "as much esteemed, loved, and cared for, as his nearest relations."

Most of the witnesses also hear a quiet warning. Christ here "put a comparative neglect upon his mother," says Henry's tradition, "which seemed purposely designed to obviate... the extravagant respect which men in aftertimes would be apt to pay her." Wesley agrees: "in this preference of his true disciples even to the Virgin Mary, considered merely as his mother after the flesh, he... seems designedly to guard against those excessive and idolatrous honours which he foresaw would in after ages be paid to her." Robertson, with tender realism, lets "one's heart go out to Mary, who has to go back home without even seeing her wondrous Son" — yet the point of the scene, the brothers being hostile, is that Christ binds to himself, by a look and a word, the family of all who hear and obey. Henry's tradition adds that "our respect ought to be guided and governed by Christ's"; since he postpones his mother to "ordinary believers," we learn both to honour those that fear the Lord and to beware the extravagant devotion that later ages would press beyond what Christ himself allowed.
35:
"For whosoever shall do the will of God, the same is my brother, and my sister, and my mother." The witnesses dwell on the bond that constitutes Christ's true family — obedience to God.

The mark of kinship is doing God's will. Gill expounds it as "believing in Christ, receiving him as a Saviour and Redeemer, and submitting to him in all his ordinances, as King of saints"; Barnes refers it to those who are doers, not hearers only (cf. James 1:25; 1 John 2:17, "he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever"). The bond is not natural but moral and spiritual, open to all who obey.

Pseudo-Jerome draws the breadth of it: "let us be assured that we are His brethren and His sisters, if we do the will of the Father; that we may be joint-heirs with Him, for He discerns us not by sex but by our deeds." The naming of "sister" as well as "brother" and "mother," the witnesses note, embraces every believer without distinction — the same range the variant reading of v.32 ("and thy sisters") had anticipated.

Henry's tradition presses the comfort home. It is "a great comfort to all true Christians, that they are dearer to Christ than mother, brother, or sister... merely as relations in the flesh would have been," and "a good reason why we should honour those that fear the Lord, and choose them for our people." Those who "not only hear, but do, the will of God" are reckoned by Christ "as much esteemed, loved, and cared for, as his nearest relations." So the chapter that began with the rulers plotting his death, and his own kin calling him mad, closes with the Lord drawing a new circle of kinship around the obedient — a family bound not by blood but by the doing of the Father's will, in which the lowliest believer is owned by Christ as brother, sister, and mother. It is good, Henry's tradition concludes, "to be akin to those who are thus nearly allied to Christ, and to have fellowship with those that have fellowship with Christ"; though his bodily presence is no longer with us, "his spiritual presence is not denied us," and this great and gracious privilege of kinship with him is ours even now.
